summary.brief: Ronnie McDaniel v. Payson Healthcare. Medical malpractice case involving surgery orthopaedic.

evidence_index.text: On July 17, 2011, Dallas Haught cut his knee in a dirt-bike accident. He went to Payson Regional Medical Center (Payson Regional), where Dr. Darnell, a surgeon affiliated with defendant Payson Healthcare Management (PHM), provided initial treatment.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Darnell ordered a series of tests, including one to measure Haught’s C-Reactive Protein (CRP) level, a marker for infection that measures inflammation. Dr. Darnell incorrectly recorded the CRP test result in Haught’s records, reflecting a reading of 45 instead of 138.79 mg/dl.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: At some point, Haught developed necrotizing fasciitis, resulting in the surgical removal of all the skin on his right leg.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The trial court correctly declined to find a violation of the One-Expert Rule.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: We affirm the trial court’s ruling concerning the testimony of the treating physicians and its denial of PHM’s motion to name Dr. Cory a nonparty at fault and remand for a new trial.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: Haught argued that by answering hypothetical questions about CRP test results and the potential impact on his treatment, the treating physicians testified as experts, which violated the One-Expert Rule.
evidence_index.speaker: Plaintiff's argument

evidence_index.text: The primary purpose of the One-Expert Rule, which is to limit the cost of the presentation of multiple retained experts.
evidence_index.speaker: Court reasoning

evidence_index.text: Dr. Sharma, an employee of 4C Medical Group (collectively “4CMG”) in Scottsdale, saw Haught on his arrival and prepared an initial report that omitted the CRP result.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The court of appeals’ resolution of the undisclosed expert opinions issue, for which we did not grant review, raises the question of whether this case should still be remanded for a new trial.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: Defendants PHM and 4CMG called Drs. Berge, Cory, Friedman, and Schaub to testify and made clear at the outset of their testimony that each was not a “paid expert.”
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient cut his knee in a dirt-bike accident and presented to Payson Regional Medical Center.

focused_exam.knee: Injury noted, specifics not detailed in judgment

initial_state.initial_working_problem: Knee injury with potential for infection

requestables.name: C-Reactive Protein (CRP)
requestables.description: Test to measure inflammation and potential infection

reveal.result_summary: CRP recorded as 45 instead of 138.79 mg/dl
reveal.clinical_significance: Incorrect recording could delay diagnosis of infection
reveal.decision_impact: Accurate result should prompt further investigation for infection

key_events.event: Initial assessment and CRP test ordered

timeline_phases.name: Further Assessment
timeline_phases.description: Patient's condition worsens, further tests considered

decision_points.prompt: What should be done after receiving the CRP test result?

options.description: Order additional imaging and repeat CRP test
options.clinical_reasoning: To confirm infection and assess severity
Assumed initial CRP result was accurate

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant transferred patient without further testing
actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Assumed initial CRP result was accurate

expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence suggested further testing was necessary

patient_outcome.description: Patient developed necrotizing fasciitis requiring extensive surgery

key_findings: Breach of duty in failing to accurately record CRP result
Causation established - delayed diagnosis led to severe outcomes

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Failure to accurately record and act on CRP result
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Accurate CRP result would have prompted further investigation

factual_timeline.event: Patient cut knee in dirt-bike accident and presented to Payson Regional Medical Center

treatments.treatment: Surgical removal of skin on right leg

allegations.allegation: Failure to accurately record CRP result
